title: Case binding, how it works
subtitle: From sewn text block to cased-in book
excerpt: Case binding is the structure of almost every commercially printed hardback book. The text block is sewn and glued into a self-contained unit, and the case, the hard cover assembly, is made separately. The two are then joined by gluing the outermost sheets of the text block (the endpapers) to the inside faces of the case boards. This article explains the logic of each stage.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapters on casing in, the text block, and the cover. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. This is the canonical Arts-and-Crafts-era bookbinding reference; Cockerell's case-binding instructions are detailed and still followed closely by hand binders today.

glossaryTerms.definition: The sewn-and-glued body of the book, comprising all signatures with their spine glued and backed, before the cover is attached.
The hard cover assembly made independently of the text block: two boards plus a spine piece, covered in book cloth or leather and glued as a unit.
The folded sheet that bridges the text block and the case. One half (the paste-down) glues to the inside face of the cover board; the other half (the flyleaf) is the first free page of the book.
Two sequential processes: rounding curves the spine of the glued text block so it tapers toward the fore-edge; backing creates a shoulder at each side of the spine that the cover boards sit against.

aliases: case bound book how it works

content.text: A case binding separates the making of the book's internal pages from the making of its cover. The
text block
, sewn signatures, glued spine, headbands, is completed as a self-contained unit. The
case
, boards, spine piece, and cover cloth, is made separately. The two come together in the final step when the
endpapers
are pasted down to the inside faces of the boards. This separation is what makes commercial book production possible: the text block can be produced in one department and the cases in another.
Stage 1: Sewing the text block

The text block is sewn in multiple signatures using any compatible stitch: sewing on tapes (for a case binding with raised cords or tape hinges), sewing on cords, or a straightforward recessed-cord sewing where the cords sit in grooves sawn into the signature folds. For home hand-binding, sewing on three to five linen tapes produces a strong joint without requiring special equipment.
Stage 2: Gluing and rounding

After sewing, the spine is glued with a thin application of PVA. While the glue is still slightly flexible (not fully dry), the spine is rounded: the spine is worked over a curved surface or with the palm, pushing the centre forward into a convex curve.
Rounding and backing
together allow the book to open cleanly at any page and create the shoulder that the boards will rest against.

For home-scale books without a backing hammer and press, a simplified approach works: glue the spine, allow to firm slightly, and gently curve it by pressing with the thumbs along the spine while holding the text block in a book press (or between two boards clamped with G-clamps). A slight curve is sufficient.
Stage 3: Headbands

In fine bookbinding, headbands are sewn onto the head and tail of the spine, a small decorative and structural element visible when a book is shelved and pulled by the top edge. For a home case binding, pre-made cotton headband cloth cut to width and glued to the spine head and tail achieves the visual effect without the complex sewing.
Stage 4: Making the case

The case consists of two cover boards, a spine piece, and a covering material. The spine piece is made from heavy card (not binders board, it needs to flex). The boards and spine piece are laid out on the covering cloth with a 3 to 5 mm gap between the board edge and the spine piece on each side: this is the hinge. The cloth is glued, the boards and spine piece are pressed into it, and the turn-ins are folded over. The case is pressed overnight.
Stage 5: Casing in

Casing in is the joining step. PVA is applied to the outside face of the front endpaper and the text block is pressed into the open case, with the spine of the text block seated against the spine piece of the case. The boards are folded closed and the book is pressed between flat boards for at least one hour. The same is done for the back endpaper. The finished book is pressed overnight before use.
What makes a good case binding

Cockerell identified three conditions for a long-lasting case binding: the paper must be long-grain parallel to the spine; the endpapers must be heavier than the text-block paper to carry the hinge stress; and the spine must be glued with a flexible adhesive (PVA or paste) that does not crack under repeated opening.
